utterances: That's Bob Saul, another Vietnam veteran, who came to hear McCain in Ohio this week.
The military should run the war.
We had problems with that in Vietnam where the politicians tried to run it from D.C., and it was obviously a very bad mistake.
Another Ohio veteran, Wendell Herrin, worries the two Democratic presidential candidates were trying to bring troops home too early.
They're not going to win that war overnight, and I think it's going to be a long haul, but I think we have to do it.
Down the hall, some graduate MBA students view the crisis through another lens.
I am pitching Sigma-Aldrich Corporation, ticker SIAL.
These are top students who manage a million-dollar investment portfolio, a pile of real money they must steer through a tanking stock market.
Professor Sarah Kroncke says it's been a rough week.
Inside this calm exterior is a very panicked individual.
(Laughing)
This group is focused on the long term.
They can talk for hours about whether to add a single stock to their portfolio.
But Ben Ju(ph), a member of the team, says this week they had to take drastic action.
Mr.
BEN JU(ph) (MBA Student, Robert H.
Smith School of Business, University of Maryland): On Tuesday, we had a relatively lengthy discussion on what to do with Merrill Lynch and Wachovia, which we ended up selling.
So with Merrill Lynch...
You sold all your holdings of those two stocks on Tuesday?
We sold both of those holdings on Tuesday.
At biz schools around the country, the bad news from Wall Street has been hard to ignore.
I would say the mood is very sober.
I think, right now, the students are shaken by these events.
Daniel Beim teaches finance at Columbia University in New York.
Beim says his students are also rethinking their career options, but he is not steering them away from finance.
Instead, he's telling them there's a lot to be learned when financial schemes designed by whiz kids go up in smoke.
[POST-BROADCAST CORRECTION: His name is DAVID Beim.]
You should be willing to stand against the crowd.
You should be willing to say, I know everybody is making a lot of money, and this looks like a great thing to do, but I'm worried that this may not make sense.
At nearby New York University, Professor Aswath Damodaran says for a long time too many students succumbed to the dream of becoming masters of the universe at the big investment firms.
We've drained corporations.
We've drained businesses of their brightest talent and brought them to New York, and London, and Tokyo, and put them in investment banks.
Now, Damodaran hopes students will go where they are needed most, to mainstream American companies that are struggling to survive in a global economy.
Larry Abramson, NPR News.
In New York, a backlash forced the state to scrap a plan to give driver's licenses to illegal immigrants.
That doesn't mean, of course, that hundreds of thousands of undocumented immigrants in the state have stopped driving.
North Country Public Radio's David Sommerstein reports from upstate New York.
A 30-year-old Mexican woman pulls into her driveway in a rural area near Syracuse.
She doesn't want to use her name because she snuck into the United States nine years ago.
She changes out of her dairy farm clothes, stinky from a 10-hour shift around manure and sour milk and into a flowing blouse and skirt.
Then she summons her two young daughters, both born here, to clean up the living room.
(Speaking in foreign language)
She says driving is a necessity out here - to get the girls to the doctor, to get to work, to get groceries.
But every errand is risky.
(Through translator) If I get stopped by the police and deported, the biggest fear is I that I'll have to leave my children.
I have that nightmare every night, I swear.
When New York Governor Eliot Spitzer announced the plan last fall to allow illegal immigrants to get driver's licenses, she gathered all the documentation she could.
(Through translator) I said the minute they say it's the law, I'll be the first at the Department of Motor Vehicles, ready to get a license.
Mostly Latino immigrants are a fixture in upstate New York's fruit, vegetable and dairy farms, and they have adapted to living life in the shadows.
Take this Honduran farm worker who also declined to use his name.
He says the police will give you a break if you have auto insurance even if you don't have a license.
(Through translator) Everyone in this community drives with insurance.
But it's insurance from someone else who's helped you out, a Puerto Rican, for example, or someone else who has papers.
Governor Spitzer argued granting immigrants licenses would shed light on who's driving in New York.
He said it would reduce hit-and-run accidents and save $120 million a year in premiums that cover the uninsured.
Immigrant groups said the plan was a step forward.
Alejandra Molina is a local professor who does advocacy work with farm worker women.
She says it may seem ironic, but by and large illegal immigrants want to do the right thing.
Overall, people are concerned about doing something that's not legal within the community.
If they could, of course, they want to be living according to the law.
But most New Yorkers didn't see it that way.
A poll found 72 percent opposed Spitzer's plan.
They shouldn't be here to begin with.
They're illegal.
They don't belong here.
Linda Matthias is having coffee at the Cascade Diner(ph) in Canton, another agricultural town in New York.
Her friend, Fritsy Kootz(ph), says she is more concerned about why Americans can't do those jobs.
I think this very unfortunate.
It's much more complex, I think, than driver's licenses.
Ontario County undersheriff, David Tillman(ph), says other illegal drivers, like those suspended for drunk driving, cause more problems than undocumented immigrants who mostly blend in.
I mean, they don't stand out as far as committing petty offenses, or having family issues or domestic disputes and so on and so forth, to any greater degree than anyone else.
You can read a victim's firsthand account of waterboarding and learn more about its use throughout history at npr.org.
Do you think that's what's driving the increased partisanship in Congress?
I think it builds on itself, all of the things.
The advent of special interests, I think, has been a major contributor on all sides to the equation.
What do you mean?

Well, when I was a young staff member, and then a junior congressman, and Marty was, too, I mean there were always lobbyists in this town, there are always going to be advocates, and some of them are paid to do their job.
But the proliferation, there are thousands of them today.
And that tends to have people think in one dimension, and not take a broader view.
You've got to be the knight in shining armor and you're going to defeat the devil.
Whether you're a group or an individual, and people have got to be, stay pure for whatever it is, or -- and the political process is where you come -- different values and different sense of values.
But then you try and find pragmatic solutions to problems with that coalition of values.
And I think they answer before they figure out even what the problem is.

The Cincinnati Reds hits a deep fly to left field and Nats outfielder Alphonzo Soriano misses the catch.
(Soundbite of booing)
Oh, Soriano.
That's two big wins he needs.
Just one run scored.
They both scored?
Yeah, they both scored.
